speaker: interviewer
text: It is now 1958, and you're released from the American Army. You're already married in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am married.

speaker: interviewer
text: To?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sally Nordenberg-- naturally became Wittelsohn. And I think I mentioned that already she found out at that point in our lives-- and I think we had been married already about four or five years-- that she herself had been a hidden child, that her mother and father was really not her mother and father. That they had all perished in-- in Poland, and that she was raised by her aunts, and given up for adoption with the parents that she thought were her biological parents. When she found that out, with the baggage that I had, and the baggage now that she was made to have, something happened to our relationship. We had two children, a boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My boy is Dean. Today, he's called Dean Wittelsohn-Steven. Last name for another story. My daughter is Deborah Lee Karen. She is married. And when she found that out, somehow, because, I suppose, what I had gone through-- and-- and I might say right now that I-- I suppose the war has done this to me. I hope I can blame it on the war. It's made me very, very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't have an awful lot of emotions that I show. I-- I was never able to do that. So I guess it stayed with me. Love was never shown to me, because we had other things to think about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm not-- I'm not very demonstrative. And I have very little patience for weakness, because the weaknesses that I hear of today, I mean, we wish we were-- those would have been a wonderful life then. And I have very little patience for-- for, actually, sicknesses, which we weren't allowed to show and all of that. And so therefore, the support that she needed probably did not come from me as it should have. And the support that I then needed was not there, because she had her own baggage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to make a long story short, the marriage failed after nine years. And we divorced. And then happily and luckily, if I may go into that, I remarried later on-- but not to a Jewish wife. In fact, I think, by design, I wasn't going to have a Jewish wife anymore. I didn't want any of this anymore. I didn't want to talk about it. I didn't want to know about it. I-- I just didn't want any part of this. I didn't want to have to explain it. I didn't want to have to talk about it. And I think that was quite safe. Although [CHUCKLES] I love my wife. We've been married 30 years, and we have two children of our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are those children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nicole Simone, and Tania Yvette-- both Wittelsohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned earlier, which I do believe is an important story to you, that you returned to Brussels 30 years later. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first, it was I thought I would never do that. I didn't want to do that. So-- but my sister had decided to go back. And well, it's a long story, but she went back for-- for her own reasons. The Barniche were still alive. She went in. She saw them. She went back to visit the convent in which she was hidden. She saw some of the sisters were still alive that had hidden her. And although my sister had 25 years of a wonderful marriage, four children of her own, well-adjusted, Americanized definitely, whatever it did to her, it destroyed her. She became obsessed with the war. She became obsessed with the Nazi. Where she had never wanted to talk about reparations, or application-- application for reparation, she became so angry, so obsessed, that's all she wanted to talk about. That's all she spoke about. And there came a time where, because of it, the marriage got very rocky. Her relationship with her children got very bad. She was cutting herself off from the rest of the world. She-- she was breaking every relationship that she had had.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1983, or '84, in the '80s, in the early '80s. And she attempted suicide. In fact, she did that five times, until she finally succeeded. And she died in 1985. And I do believe that this was because she relived everything. And instead of being therapeutic, as it was for me, it destroyed her. And her anger got to her, and she couldn't live with it anymore. And she committed suicide. So it's never really over.

speaker: interviewer
text: I, because of her visit, had a long decision. And it took me a long time to decide that, did I want to go through that? Would it then also destroy me as it destroyed her? Although I felt myself quite well adjusted, being able to take everything, had a happy marriage, four kids, everything was wonderful. I had a good life. I had made something of myself. I-- I believe I was in business for myself at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I decided I-- I just-- I had to do it. Something drew me there. So I took my wife and a couple friends of mine that knew somewhat of my background, but not the details of it. My wife knew very little of the details of my background. And we went back. And my idea was strictly to go in front of that house, just to pass it by, just to point it out, and see that it was still there, and not to go in, and certainly not to see the Barniche, who, I mean, as far as I was concerned, I had-- there were no thanks that I wanted to give to them, although, still, they are responsible for my being alive today, one way or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as I passed the house, Jeanne Barniche happened to have opened a window. She saw me. And I don't know how she did that, because, at that point, it was some 30 years later. She looked at me. I looked at her. And she said, Henri? And I said, Jeanne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she ran down. She insisted that I came-- I come in with Lorraine, my wife, and my friends. And walked in, we sat down. We-- she proceeded, by the way, and he proceeded to tell them-- my friends-- in French, because they did not speak any English, and that was a good thing, I guess, how they saved our lives, and how wonderful they were to Jews and so on. And they asked me to translate. And, of course, this was not exactly my translation to my friends and wife. I told exactly what had happened and where. It so happens that, as I said earlier, that the attic was still as it was when we left. Their hiding place under the stairway was still there. I was able to show that to my wife and my friends. And the-- the therapeutic part and the healing part was that fleur-de-lis, and the remembrance of that little toy. And all of those things did me so much good. I went back to the schools-- the Catholic school that I attended that had been bombed twice. And I've come back with such a release and such great feelings that I didn't have to hide much anymore. And of course, I didn't go into the details on how I survived. But I was able to show. I was able to be there. I was able to share it with my wife and friends. Until 1991 Hidden Children Conference, which opened up the rest of the world to people that cared and understood. And I was able to share my experiences as I'm doing now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's been 50 years after the war has ended, is there a message you would like to leave your children and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message and the important thing is never to forget. And that's primary. Never to let the world, and the people, and the governments, and the revisionists that we have today. I'm not so worried about skinheads and so on, the young punks that don't know what it was. They don't have had the slightest idea what they believe in. But revisionists scare me, people like professors and historians and politicians, whether they be from other governments or even Americans, for that matter, that say that it couldn't happen. It didn't happen. It was not possible statistically and theoretically. I want my children to know that it did happen, that this is not hearsay. I saw it. It was there. It happened. They killed 6 million Jews. And they didn't do it alone. They did it with the help of a lot of governments today that are calling themselves innocents, and like to cleanse themselves with reparations, and help, and so on. That this could happen again. Not to have complacency for-- for the [INAUDIBLE], the poor children, for example, that is happening in Bosnia right now. To look at it. It's happening all over again. Ethnic cleansing is exactly what happened during the Holocaust. They wanted to get rid of a people. And this is what they want to do today. And if we let them, if we don't pay attention, if we let it just go on, and don't believe that this is possible or that it happened, it can happen again. And I want my children to know that story, to continue that story, to tell it to their children and to their children's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you for sharing your story with us. Henri, would you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd love to. My wife of 30 years, Lorraine, who has lived through a lot of my problems. And my daughter Tania, 25 years old, who's also had to really deal with an awful lot of baggage that I bring to this marriage. But I guess it worked, since we do still love each other. And after 30 years, I can say that she's been the greatest support, both of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lorraine, is there something you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's-- it's been an incredible learning part of my life, sharing my life with Henry. And certainly it's-- it's made me so cognizant of things that I would have never been aware of. And I'm glad that I was able to share it, and be with him through this part of his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tania.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad's had a rough life. But he's made it, and I'm glad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has this affected anything you do, or how you lead your life right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I was taught pretty well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's going to learn a lot more this weekend with the Hidden Children Conference, where she finally decided that she wanted to know more about me. And that's going to be, I think, very good and very nice. And I'm glad to hear that she's interested enough, which they had not been, and I wasn't quite willing to share that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it should be a very, very therapeutic weekend for all of us, including my other daughter, who's unable to be here this morning, and two other children, who geographically would have been unable to do it. Is there anything, Henry, you would like to add that we have not spoken about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, except for one thing, to say that I suppose through all of this, I have to consider myself a very, very lucky person. There are 6 million Jews today that would like to be able to tell a story, and they're unable to. So I guess I have to tell it for them. But particularly, particularly, this 1 and 1/2 million children I keep on mentioning that are not here to be able to tell their story. And if I've accomplished anything, if I've done anything to help future generations for this not to happen, then I'm glad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father on the left. And this was taken in Brussels, Belgium in the 1930s-- late 1930s. He was then 37, and was shot at 38. My mother was a couple of years older than he was. And his name, I'm sorry, is Szmul, which-- which translates as Samuel-- Jacob Wittelsohn. My mother, her name was Bajla Kempinski-- Bajla Wittelsohn later. She was, in that picture, probably around 39 or 40 years old, also taken in Brussels, Belgium at the age of about 40. This is my brother [? Mendel ?]. He-- well, he died when he was approximately 20 to 22 years old. So I believe, at-- in that picture, he must have been 18 or 19, and was part of a twin with a sister. This is [? Minda. ?] She was then approximately 16, 17 years old in that picture. Was born in 1925, I believe, and also died in a concentration camp at the age of anywhere between 17 and 19. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] The picture was also taken in Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next picture would be my sister [? Anna. ?] This picture is of your sister, who was a twin [? to Mendel? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [? Minda ?] was not the twin. Anna would have been, which will be shown later, I imagine. [? Minda ?] was the younger-- the middle of the two sisters. She must have been about 16, 17 on that picture. It was taken in Brussels, Belgium. And it would have to have been before 1942 before she was deported, so-- and certainly before the Germans invaded. So I would say about 1940. This is a picture of Anna, who was a twin to [? Mendel. ?] She was born also in 1924, had to be about 18, 19. She was my older sister. And that picture, which was can also in Brussels, Belgium. And, of course, was taken before the war, around 1940. This picture is of my brother [? Victor. ?] He must have been no more than about 15 in that picture in Brussels, Belgium. He was the one that was taken away later on in the story, and had to have died somewhere between 16 and 18 years old. The picture must have also been taken around 1940. To the left is my oldest daughter Debbie. She will be 38 this year. Her name is Debbie Karen. She was the daughter of my first marriage. Then there's my son Dean Steven-- Dean Wittelsoh-Steven-- who is going to be 35 this year. He was also the son of my first marriage. On the upper end to the left is my daughter Nicole Simone Wittelsohn, who is 28 this year, and is the daughter of my second marriage with Lorraine. And to the upper right is Tania Yvette Wittelsohn, who is 25 this year, and is also the daughter of my second marriage to Lorraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1990 as a holiday gift for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Jersey, I believe. This is my grandson Matthew Wittelsohn-Steven, who is-- was 10 years old this year. This picture was taken in New Jersey in 1995. And he's the son of my son Dean. This is a picture of my granddaughter Alexa Lauren Karen. She is the daughter of my daughter Deborah. This picture was taken in New Jersey when she was six months old. And as I said, she just celebrated her first birthday yesterday, October the 4th, 1995.
